frameworks.name: representational drift
frameworks.one_line: sensory neural representations change systematically across time

frameworks.name: image-computable encoding model
frameworks.one_line: voxelwise model fit per session to track cross-session generalization

frameworks.name: representational dissimilarity analysis
frameworks.one_line: population-level stimulus geometry tested for stability across sessions

frameworks.name: stable downstream read-out hypothesis
frameworks.one_line: downstream areas may read stable code despite V1 drift

core_question: Do stimulus representations in human primary visual cortex remain stable across months, or do they drift?
key_assumptions: fMRI responses to natural scenes index V1 stimulus representation
encoding-model fits generalize across sessions if representations are stable
longitudinal within-subject design isolates drift from inter-subject variance
manipulations: session separation (across many months within subject)
natural-scene image identity across repeated viewings
key_findings: encoding-model cross-session generalization degraded cumulatively with time
neural responsivity changes were the source of the drift
population-level representational dissimilarities between stimuli were unchanged
interpretations: human V1 exhibits drift even though rodent-like stable code was expected
downstream cortex may read out a stable representation despite drifting V1 responses
